coredata.dc:title: Electrical spin switch in a two-electron triangular graphene quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Qingrui
coredata.dc:description:
We study a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell. The electronic structures of the dot are calculated as a function of an electric field using a combination of tight-binding, Hartree–Fock and exact-diagonalization methods. To use the total spin ( S ) of the dot as a qubit, we pay attention to the energy difference between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states. The energy difference increases unexpectedly up to a rather large value before a crossing between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states occurs. To understand the unexpected change, we propose an approach to analyze the total interaction energy of the two-electron states. As the electric field changes, the total interaction energies of the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states exhibit three characteristics in turn: oscillation, transition and stability. Our findings support the possibility to use a triangular graphene quantum dot as a qubit of a carbon-based quantum computer.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Schematic picture of a TGQD (
N
e d g e
= 8) subjected to the electric field generated by two gates with opposite electrostatic potentials
± U
. The contour of the electrostatic potential is shown by dashed curves. (b) Evolution of the degenerate shell with the electric field. The energy gap between the shell and the lower band is more than 0.45 eV.
Low-lying two-electron spectra as a function of U . Inset: Zooming into the weak- U region reveals anticrossings between the energy levels.

(a) Energy difference
Δ E
, (b) interaction energies
E
S
i n t
, (c) energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
and (d) energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
as functions of the electric field.

head.ce:title: Electrical spin switch in a two-electron triangular graphene quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong, 250014, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong, 250014, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We study a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell. The electronic structures of the dot are calculated as a function of an electric field using a combination of tight-binding, Hartree–Fock and exact-diagonalization methods. To use the total spin ( S ) of the dot as a qubit, we pay attention to the energy difference between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states. The energy difference increases unexpectedly up to a rather large value before a crossing between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states occurs. To understand the unexpected change, we propose an approach to analyze the total interaction energy of the two-electron states. As the electric field changes, the total interaction energies of the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states exhibit three characteristics in turn: oscillation, transition and stability. Our findings support the possibility to use a triangular graphene quantum dot as a qubit of a carbon-based quantum computer.

ce:section.ce:para:
The electron spin is a very excellent candidate for a solid-state qubit [1] and several major experimental breakthroughs have been achieved using semiconductor quantum dots [2–5]. It is still an enormous challenge to fabricate a spin-based quantum computer in solid-state platforms. One of the main problems is the upper limit of spin coherence times due to the hyperfine interaction of the electron spin with the surrounding nuclear spins [6–8]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop qubits in quantum dots based on other materials, where hyperfine interaction is very weaker [9]. It is well known that carbon-based materials such as graphene are promising candidates. This is because natural carbon is composed mainly of the zero-spin isotope
12
C
, for which the hyperfine interaction is absent. Several studies have been performed on forming qubits in graphene quantum dots (GQDs), such as coupled qubits [10], spin qubits in quantum antidots [11] and electrical spin manipulation [12].

It is necessary for making qubits in GQDs to comprehend the electronic structures of these GQDs. The electronic and magnetic properties of GQDs depend strongly on the type of edges [13]. For triangular GQDs (TGQDs) with zigzag edges, there appears a degenerate shell of electronic states well separated in energy from both the valence and conduction bands [14]. The development of nanofabrication techniques [15–17] triggered a lot of theoretical studies on the degenerate shell, including electronic structures [18–21], transport properties [22–24] and external field control [25–32]. For the fractionally filled degenerate shell, the strong correlation effect can be accurately calculated using the configuration-interaction method [20] or the unbiased density-matrix renormalization group algorithm [33], which is a prerequisite for related device applications.

In this paper, we concentrate on a TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell and aim to form a qubit in the TGQD. We pay attention to the energy difference
Δ E
between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states as a function of an external electric field, which determines the ground-state spin of the system. We have previously attempt to switch the ground-state spin by an external electric field [24]. The energy difference
Δ E
was found to increase unexpectedly with increasing electric field and the spin switch could not obtained. In the present work, by simply increasing the strength of the electric field, we obtain a singlet-triplet spin switch in the TGQD. The real difficulty is how to explain the change of the energy difference
Δ E
with the electric field. To overcome this difficulty, we propose an approach to analyze the total interaction energy of the two-electron states.

Fig. 1(a) shows the geometrical structure of a TGQD with the size
N
e d g e
= 8, where
N
e d g e
is the number of carbon atoms in each edge of the dot. The total number of carbon atoms in the TGQD is
[
(
N
e d g e
+ 2 )
2
− 3 ]
= 97. The TGQD is subjected to an in-plane electric field generated by two gate electrodes with opposite electrostatic potentials
± U
. For the charged system with a fully occupied valence band and a completely empty degenerate shell, the quasi-particle states
ϕ
i
( r )
and the corresponding energies
ϵ
i
can be obtained by using a combination of tight-binding and Hartree–Fock methods. The corresponding Hamiltonian can be expressed as [13]
(1)
H =
∑
i σ
U
i
c
i σ
+
c
i σ
+
∑
i l σ
t
i l
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
+
∑
i l σ
∑
j k
σ
′
(
ρ
j k
σ
′
−
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
)
(
〈 i j
| V |
k l 〉
−
〈 i j
| V |
l k 〉
δ
σ
σ
′
)
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
where
U
i
is the gate electrostatic potential at site i ,
c
i σ
+
(
c
i σ
) creates (annihilates) an electron with spin σ ,
t
i l
is a hopping integral,
ρ
j k
σ
′
is the density matrix element for the TGQD,
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
is for the charge-neutral bulk graphene, and
〈 i j
| V |
k l 〉
is the two-body Coulomb matrix element. Due to the homogeneous geometrical configuration, the hopping integral may be taken as
t
i l
= − 2 . 7
eV
between nearest neighbors and
t
i l
= − 0 . 1
eV
between next-nearest neighbors [13]. The scattering and exchange interaction terms were calculated within nearest neighbors previously in Ref. [24]. Here, we calculate these scattering and exchange interaction terms within next-nearest neighbors and expect to get more accurate results. Details of the HF self-consistent computational procedure can be found in Ref. [13]. The HF energy spectra as a function of U are shown in Fig. 1(b). The electrostatic potential U = 1.0 eV corresponds to the maximum electric field strength 1.0 V/nm. Within the range of U under investigation, the energy gap between the shell and the lower (higher) band is more than 0.45 eV (0.75 eV). The magnitude of the energy gaps implies that the shell is still well separated in energy from both the valence and conduction bands. Later, these HF single-electron states
ϕ
i
( r )
will be used to construct the basis set for calculating the two-electron states.

We model the TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell by the Hamiltonian
(2)
H =
H
1
+
H
2
+
V
i n t
(
|
r
2
−
r
1
|
)
,
where
H
1
(
H
2
) is the single-electron Hamiltonian with the formalism of Eq. (1) and
V
i n t
(
|
r
2
−
r
1
|
)
is the interaction between two electrons with the formalism in Ref. [20]. Based on obtaining the single-electron states
ϕ
i
( r )
, the two-electron states with the total spin S can be expressed as [34]
(3)
Ψ
S
(
r
1
,
r
2
)
=
∑
i ⩽ j
α
i , j
{
ϕ
i
(
r
1
)
ϕ
j
(
r
2
)
+
( − 1 )
S
ϕ
i
(
r
2
)
ϕ
j
(
r
1
)
}
,
where
α
i , j
is the component coefficient of a two-electron configuration. The eigenstates and the corresponding energies can be obtained by the exact diagonalization of the Hamiltonian matrix. In our calculations, the two-electron configuration in Eq. (3) is consisting of only the eigenstates
ϕ
i
( r )
from the degenerate shell. Here, the number of eigenstates is 28 for S =0 and 21 for S =1.

Fig. 2 displays the low-lying two-electron spectra of the TGQD as a function of U . For
U < 0 . 5
eV
some details of the spectra are different from the results in Ref. [24], which is due to the improvement of accuracy in the calculation of the exchange and scattering interaction terms. For
U < 0 . 1
eV
there are many anticrossings between the levels of the same S , which implies that the corresponding eigenstates mix drastically with each other. For
U > 0 . 1
eV
there are not anticrossings between the low-lying
S = 1
levels and these levels are almost linear with U , which implies that the corresponding eigenstates keep stable [35]. By contrast, there are still an anticrossing between the two lowest
S = 0
levels stretching in a wider range of U , which implies that the two lowest
S = 0
eigenstates still mix slowly with each other.

In the two-electron spectra, we are most concerned about the energy difference
Δ E
between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states, which determines the ground-state spin of the TGQD. Fig. 3(a) displays the energy difference
Δ E
as a function of U . At
U = 0
, the
S = 1
state is lower than the
S = 0
state. The reason seems to be that the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
between the two lowest single-electron states is smaller than the exchange energy. As the electric field increases, the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
also increases [Fig. 1(b) or Fig. 3(c)]. Thus, we expect that the energy difference
Δ E
always decreases as the electric field becomes stronger. However, the energy difference
Δ E
increases unexpectedly up to about 40 meV after a little decrease. Fortunately, for
U > 0 . 5
eV
the energy difference
Δ E
decreases steadily and at
U ≈ 0 . 82
eV
there is a desired crossing between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states.

To explain the change of the energy difference
Δ E
with U , we now turn our attention to Eqs. (2)–(3). Eq. (3) shows that each two-electron state is the superposition of all single-electron states. For the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states
Ψ
S
0
, the energy of the two non-interaction electrons can be expressed as
(4)
〈

S

As U increases, the energy spacing of the single-electron states can become enough large [Fig. 1(b)]. Meanwhile, the lowest
S = 0
state
Ψ
S = 0
0
tends to be the superposition of two lowest single-electron states
ϕ
1
while the lowest
S = 1
state
Ψ
S = 1
0
tends to be the superposition of the single-electron states
ϕ
1
and
ϕ
2
. So, the state
Ψ
S = 0
0
and the corresponding energy
E
S = 0
0
can be expressed as
(6)
Ψ
S = 0

ϕ

)

Ψ

,

S = 0
is the direct interaction energy between the two electrons. Similarly, the state

and the corresponding energy

(7)

)

,
where
D
S = 1
and
K
S = 1
are the direct and exchange interaction energy between the two electrons, respectively.

In the following, we propose an approach to analyze the two-electron energies
E
S = 0
0
and
E
S = 1
0
. Whether U is strong or weak, we assume that each of the two electron filled in the shell only occupy one certain single-electron eigenstate. So, each two-electron state
Ψ
S
is the superposition of only one or two certain single-electron eigenstates,
Ψ
S
(
r
1
,
r
2
)
=
ϕ
i
(
r
1
)
ϕ
j
(
r
2
)
+
( − 1 )
S
ϕ
i
(
r
2
)
ϕ
j
(
r
1
)
.
Based on this assumption, the two-electron energy
E
S = 0
0
(
E
S = 1
0
) consists of two parts: the energies of two non-interaction electrons
2
ϵ
1
(
ϵ
1
+
ϵ
2
) and the interaction energy between the two electrons
D
S = 0
(
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
). For each two-electron state, the assumption neglects the superposition of the other single-electron states. If the energy spacing of the single-electron states is not enough large, this assumption can lead to wrong two-electron eigenfunctions. Eq. (3) implies that each wrong two-electron eigenfunction can be corrected by adding the superposition of those neglected single-electron states. According to configuration interaction theory [36], the corresponding correction to the energies
E
S = 0
0
and
E
S = 1
0
is that the two energy levels are subjected to a repulsion from the other two-electron energy levels. The repulsive interaction energy is denoted by
−
E
S = 0
e x
(
−
E
S = 1
e x
) for the lowest
S = 0
(
S = 1
) energy level, where the negative sign indicates a decrease in energy.

The above analysis approach decomposes the energy
E
S = 0
0
into three parts: (1) the energy of the two non-interacting electrons,
2
ϵ
1
; (2) the internal interaction energy between the two electrons,
D
S = 0
; (3) the repulsive interaction energy,
−
E
S = 0
e x
. Thus, we get
(8)
E
S = 0
0
= 2
ϵ
1
+
D
S = 0
−
E
S = 0
e x
.
Similarly, the lowest
S = 1
energy level can be written as
(9)
E
S = 1
0
=
ϵ
1
+
ϵ
2
+
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
−
E
S = 1
e x
.
It should be noted that this decomposition is only used for understanding the two-electron energies
E
S = 0
0
and
E
S = 1
0
and does not change the calculation procedure. In addition, the analysis approach is applicable to all two-electron systems with degenerate energy levels, not just the TGQD. According to Eqs. (8)–(9), the total interaction energies can be expressed as
(10)
E
S = 0
i n t
=
D
S = 0
−
E
S = 0
e x
and
(11)
E
S = 1
i n t
=
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
−
E
S = 1
e x
.
The energy difference
Δ E
can be expressed as
(12)
Δ E =
E
S = 0
0
−
E
S = 1
0
=
(
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
)
−
(
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
)
.
Fig. 3(c) shows that the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
increase mostly linearly. So, according to Eq. (12), to explain the change of the energy difference
Δ E
, we need to focus on the change of the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
[Fig. 3(d)]. As we will see, the total interaction energies of the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states exhibit three characteristics in turn: oscillation, transition and stability [Fig. 3(b)].

At
U = 0
, both
E
S = 0
i n t
and
E
S = 1
i n t
are near to zero [Fig. 3(b)]. Thus, according to Eqs. (10)–(11), we get
D
S = 0
≈
E
S = 0
e x
and
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
≈
E
S = 1
e x
.
According to Eqs. (6)–(7),
D
S = 0
≫
D
S = 1
,
and further
D
S = 0
≫
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
.
So,
E
S = 0
e x
≫
E
S = 1
e x
.
The above analysis shows that the contribution of the internal interaction energy
D
S = 0
(
D
S = 1
−
K
S = 1
) to the total interaction energy
E
S = 0
i n t
(
E
S = 1
i n t
) is almost canceled out by that of the repulsive interaction energy
−
E
S = 0
e x
(
−
E
S = 1
e x
). In Fig. 3, at
U = 0
, the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
is about 20 meV and the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
is about 5 meV. So, according to Eq. (12), the energy difference
Δ E
is about 15 meV.

For
U < 0 . 02
eV
, the interaction energy
E
S = 0
i n t
increases more slowly than the interaction energy
E
S = 1
i n t
[Fig. 3(b)] and thus the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
drops down [Fig. 3(d)]. According to Eq. (12), the energy difference
Δ E
drops down [Fig. 3(a)]. At
U ≈ 0 . 02
eV
, the energy
E
S = 0
i n t
begins to increase faster than the energy
E
S = 1
i n t
and thus the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
begins to increase. Moreover, the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
begins to increase faster than the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
. So, according to Eq. (12), the energy difference
Δ E
begins to increase.

Within the region (
0 ≤ U < 0 . 1
eV
), the two-electron states mixes with each other dramatically and randomly as well as the single-electron states. Thus, the interaction energy oscillates randomly as U changes, although there is only one oscillation peak.

For
U > 0 . 1
eV
, the two lowest single-electron levels of the shell are linear with U [Fig. 1(b)], which implies that the corresponding single-electron states no longer changes significantly [35]. The stability of the single-electron eigenstates causes two consequences: (1) the internal interaction energies
D
S = 0
,
D
S = 1
and
K
S = 1
tend to be stable; (2) the change of the total interaction energy
E
S = 0
i n t
(
E
S = 1
i n t
) is mainly due to the change of the repulsive interaction energy
E
S = 0
e x
(
E
S = 1
e x
). The interaction energy
E
S = 0
e x
(
E
S = 1
e x
) decreases as the energy spacing of the two-electron states increases. For
U > 0 . 1
eV
, the interaction energy
E
S = 0
e x
decreases faster than
E
S = 1
e x
and thus
E
S = 0
i n t
increases faster than
E
S = 1
i n t
. In Eq. (12), the term
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
increases faster than the term
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
and thus the energy difference
Δ E
continues to increase [Fig. 1(a)].

At
U ≈ 0 . 2
eV
, the interaction energy
E
S = 1
i n t
tends to be stable [Fig. 3(b)], which implies that the interaction energy
E
S = 1
e x
tends to be zero. Namely, the energy difference between the
S = 1
ground and excited states is large enough that
E
S = 1
e x
can be ignored. The subsequent decrease in the energy level
E
S = 1
0
is only due to the decrease in the energy sum
ϵ
1
+
ϵ
2
and thus the energy level
E
S = 1
0
is linear with U [Fig. 2]. For
0 . 2 < U < 0 . 8
eV
, the interaction energy
E
S = 0
i n t
and the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
increase more and more slowly, which implies that the interaction energy
E
S = 0
e x
drops more and more slowly. At
U ≈ 0 . 5
eV
, the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
increases as fast as the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
and according to Eq. (12) the energy difference
Δ E
reaches the maximum value.

For
U > 0 . 5
eV
, the energy difference
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
increases more slowly than the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
. Thus the energy difference
Δ E
drops down [Fig. 3(a)]. At
U ≈ 0 . 82
eV
, the energy difference
Δ E
tends to be zero. At
U ≈ 0 . 8
eV
, the interaction energy
E
S = 0
i n t
tends to be stable [Fig. 3(b)], which implies that the interaction energy
E
S = 0
e x
also tends to be zero. According to Eq. (8), the subsequent decrease in the energy
E
S = 0
0
is only due to the decrease in the energy
ϵ
1
, which is also proved by the linearity of the energy level
E
S = 0
0
[Fig. 2].

Within the region (
0 . 1 < U < 0 . 8
eV
), the energy spacing of the single-electron levels increases gradually and the interaction among the different two-electron states decays. Meanwhile, the change of the total interaction energies with U transits from the oscillation region to the stability region.

For
0 . 8 < U < 1 . 0
eV
, the interaction energies
E
S = 0
i n t
and
E
S = 1
i n t
keep stable and the change of
Δ E
is only due to the change of the energy difference
ϵ
2
−
ϵ
1
. Meanwhile,
E
S = 0
0
,
E
S = 1
0
and
Δ E
are all almost linear with U .

To switch the ground-state spin in a two-electron system, the energy difference between two lowest single-electron states cannot be too large, or the lowest
S = 0
energy level is always lower than the lowest
S = 1
energy level. The degenerate shell of TGQDs can satisfy this requirement very well. However, for the shell, there is another trouble that the single-electron energy levels are too close to each other. This trouble results in the strong interaction among the two-electron eigenstates, which can delay the switch of the ground-state spin, as we have seen before.

Fortunately, an external electric field can lead to the splitting of the degenerate shell. Meanwhile the interaction among the two-electron states can decay and then a crossing between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states can occur. The finding supports the possibility to form a qubit in the TGQD. The energy difference
Δ E
for the TGQD is approximately linear with the electric field near the switch point and the maximum value of
Δ E
is about 40 meV, which are helpful to the operation of the TGQD as a qubit.

In conclusion, we have studied the influence of the in-plane electric field on the electric structure of the TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell. The singlet-triplet spin switch can be obtained by simply increasing the strength of the electric field. As the electric field is increased, the energy difference between the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
stars from a little drop, increases up to a rather large value and then drop down to a negative value. To understand the change of the energy difference, an approach is presented to analyze the total interaction energy of the two-electron states. The interaction-energy analysis approach is applicable to all two-electron systems with degenerate energy levels. As the electric field changes, the total interaction energies of the lowest
S = 0
and
S = 1
states exhibit three characteristics in turn: oscillation, transition and stability. Our findings support the possibility to use a TGQD as a qubit of a carbon-based quantum computer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11604183 and 11674197), and a Project of Shandong Province, People’s Republic of China Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LJ09).
